title: Involuntary Psychiatric Commitment, Patient Rights, and the Duty to Warn/Protect (Tarasoff) in a 34-Year-Old Voluntarily Admitted Man with Paranoid Schizophrenia Who Voices a Specific, Credible Threat to Kill an Identifiable Former Coworker, Demands to Leave, and Refuses His Antipsychotic: Balancing Confidentiality Against the Duty to Protect a Third Party, Converting a Voluntary to an Involuntary Hold (Danger to Others), and Upholding the Retained Right to Refuse Non-Emergency Medication
subtitle: This case centers on the ETHICAL and LEGAL framework that governs psychiatric care when a patient is potentially dangerous, and it deliberately turns on DANGER TO OTHERS (distinct from the danger-to-self suicide scenarios). The nurse faces a DUAL OBLIGATION — to care for and protect the PATIENT, and to take reasonable steps to PROTECT a potential VICTIM. DUTY TO WARN / DUTY TO PROTECT (the TARASOFF doctrine, Tarasoff v. Regents of the University of California, 1976) holds that when a patient communicates a SERIOUS, CREDIBLE threat of harm to a REASONABLY IDENTIFIABLE third party, the clinician's duty of confidentiality YIELDS and the treatment team must take reasonable protective steps — which may include WARNING the intended victim, NOTIFYING LAW ENFORCEMENT, and/or HOSPITALIZING the patient. As the court framed it, 'the protective privilege ends where the public peril begins.' Confidentiality (and HIPAA) PERMITS disclosure to prevent serious, imminent harm, but disclosure must be LIMITED to what is necessary to protect — NOT a broadcast of the patient's entire history. VOLUNTARY vs INVOLUNTARY admission: a VOLUNTARY patient consents to treatment and may request discharge, but if, on evaluation, the patient meets COMMITMENT CRITERIA the facility may place a short legal HOLD rather than release them. Civil-commitment criteria (which vary by state but share a common core) are: (1) DANGER TO SELF, (2) DANGER TO OTHERS, or (3) GRAVELY DISABLED (unable to meet basic needs for food/clothing/shelter) due to a mental disorder. An emergency hold (e.g., a 72-hour hold; California's 5150) allows brief involuntary detention and evaluation; continued involuntary commitment requires DUE PROCESS — certification, legal representation, and a court hearing/periodic review (habeas corpus). CRITICALLY, INVOLUNTARY COMMITMENT IS NOT THE SAME AS INCOMPETENCE: a committed patient is presumed to RETAIN decision-making capacity and a long list of RIGHTS — the right to humane care and TREATMENT, the LEAST RESTRICTIVE environment, to be informed of their rights, to communicate with others (visitors/phone/mail, within reasonable limits), to legal counsel and periodic judicial review, to dignity and privacy, freedom from unnecessary restraint/seclusion, and — high-yield — the RIGHT TO REFUSE non-emergency MEDICATION. A committed patient may refuse psychotropic medication; EMERGENCY medication over objection is permissible ONLY to prevent IMMINENT danger to self or others and must be the least restrictive effective intervention, whereas ROUTINE/ongoing medication over objection requires a separate JUDICIAL determination (a court order / due process), not merely the existence of the hold. RECOGNITION/ASSESSMENT in this scenario: the patient voices a SPECIFIC threat with intent, a plan, and identified means against a clearly identifiable person; he is agitated, paranoid, demands to leave (he is a voluntary admission), and refuses his oral antipsychotic. The nurse must also assess for and help rule out a treatable/organic or substance-related contributor. IMMEDIATE MANAGEMENT: (1) ensure SAFETY using the LEAST RESTRICTIVE effective measures (verbal DE-ESCALATION, increased/1:1 observation, a safe environment, and preventing elopement so he cannot leave to act on the threat) — restraint/seclusion only for imminent danger when less restrictive measures fail; (2) immediately NOTIFY the psychiatrist/provider and treatment team of the credible threat — do NOT sit on it under the banner of confidentiality; (3) INITIATE the involuntary hold because he meets 'danger to others' criteria (he cannot simply be allowed to leave); (4) ensure the DUTY TO WARN/PROTECT is carried out per law/policy — warn the identifiable victim and/or notify law enforcement, disclosing only what is necessary; (5) UPHOLD the patient's retained RIGHTS — inform him of his rights, honor his right to REFUSE non-emergency medication (offer, do not force; reserve medication over objection for imminent danger; seek a court order for ongoing involuntary treatment), preserve communication and legal-review rights, and use the least restrictive plan; (6) DOCUMENT objectively and contemporaneously (the threat in the patient's words, the risk assessment, who was notified and when, interventions, and the patient's rights) and continue THERAPEUTIC engagement. PERTINENT MISTAKES TO AVOID: letting a voluntary patient who meets commitment criteria leave and saying nothing; staying silent about a credible threat to an identifiable victim because of 'confidentiality'; forcibly secluding/medicating a patient solely for refusing an oral dose when there is no imminent danger and no court order; treating the hold as automatic incompetence and overriding all of the patient's choices; or over-disclosing the patient's history beyond what protection requires. NURSING ROLE: recognize the credible threat to an identifiable victim, maintain safety with the least restrictive measures, escalate to the team so the duty to warn/protect is fulfilled, support conversion to an appropriate involuntary hold (danger to others), and act as the patient's ADVOCATE by upholding the rights that civil commitment does NOT take away — especially the right to refuse non-emergency medication — while documenting objectively.

patient.name: D. Bauer
patient.code: Full code; on an inpatient behavioral-health unit; admitted VOLUNTARILY but now meeting INVOLUNTARY-hold criteria (danger to others); goal = protect the identifiable third party (duty to warn/protect), maintain safety with the least restrictive measures, convert to an appropriate involuntary hold, and uphold the patient's retained rights — including the right to refuse non-emergency medication
patient.admitDate: Known schizophrenia (paranoid features), VOLUNTARILY admitted 3 days ago for a medication adjustment after worsening paranoia. During a 1:1 interaction he becomes agitated and states a SPECIFIC, credible plan and intent to kill a former coworker he names — whom he believes is 'controlling his thoughts' — describing how he will obtain a firearm after discharge. He then DEMANDS to leave (he is a voluntary patient) and REFUSES his oral antipsychotic. The nurse must balance confidentiality against the duty to warn/protect an identifiable victim, prevent him from leaving to act on the threat, support conversion to an involuntary hold, and uphold the rights he retains.
patient.room: Inpatient behavioral-health unit; 1:1 observation initiated; interdisciplinary team (psychiatrist, psychiatric RN, social work, hospital security/risk management/legal, patient-rights advocate); de-escalation in progress

vitals.temp: 37.0°C; agitated, pacing, guarded/paranoid; states a SPECIFIC intent + plan + identified means to kill a NAMED former coworker; this serious, credible threat to an identifiable victim triggers the DUTY TO WARN/PROTECT and re-evaluation of hold status

37.0°C; demands immediate discharge (he is a VOLUNTARY admission) and REFUSES his oral antipsychotic; because he now meets 'danger to others' criteria he cannot simply be released → conversion to an involuntary hold is initiated; there is NO imminent assault at this instant, so this is not grounds to forcibly medicate

vitals.time: Provider notified + duty to warn + hold initiated
vitals.temp: 37.0°C; psychiatrist/team notified immediately; DUTY TO WARN/PROTECT carried out per law/policy (identifiable victim warned and law enforcement notified, disclosing only what is necessary); INVOLUNTARY emergency hold initiated for danger to others; 1:1 observation; least-restrictive measures; patient informed of his rights

37.0°C; verbal de-escalation effective — NO restraint or seclusion needed (least restrictive); PRN medication OFFERED and declined, and his right to refuse non-emergency medication is respected (no court order yet, no imminent danger); communication and legal-review rights preserved

37.0°C; commitment certification + court review/hearing scheduled with legal representation; if ongoing refusal plus continued danger, a SEPARATE court order for involuntary medication would be sought (the hold alone does not authorize it); safety maintained; therapeutic alliance building

37.0°C; over the following days, with engagement the patient VOLUNTARILY accepts medication, the paranoia and threat resolve, and the victim's safety is confirmed; his rights were respected throughout; documentation complete; team debrief held

labs.n: PRESENTATION + the dual duty (protect the patient AND protect a third party)
labs.v: 34 y/o man D. Bauer, known schizophrenia (paranoid features), VOLUNTARILY admitted 3 days ago for a medication adjustment. During a 1:1 he becomes agitated and voices a SPECIFIC, credible threat — intent, a plan, and identified means — to kill a NAMED former coworker he believes is controlling his thoughts, then DEMANDS to leave and REFUSES his oral antipsychotic. This creates a DUAL OBLIGATION for the team: to protect and treat the PATIENT, and to take reasonable steps to protect the identifiable potential VICTIM. The combination of a credible threat to an identifiable person, a voluntary patient who now meets commitment criteria, and a medication refusal puts three legal/ethical threads in play at once: (1) the DUTY TO WARN/PROTECT (Tarasoff) versus confidentiality; (2) conversion from VOLUNTARY to INVOLUNTARY status (danger to others); and (3) the patient's RETAINED RIGHTS, including the right to refuse non-emergency medication. The nurse must also assess for a treatable/organic or substance-related contributor (intoxication/withdrawal, metabolic derangement) as part of the workup. PMH: schizophrenia, prior admissions; SOCIAL: estranged from the named coworker; partner not present. The immediate priorities: keep everyone safe with the least restrictive measures, prevent elopement, escalate to the psychiatrist/team so the duty to protect is fulfilled, support an appropriate involuntary hold, and uphold the rights commitment does NOT remove — while documenting objectively.
labs.r: Credible threat (intent + plan + means) to an IDENTIFIABLE victim by a voluntarily admitted psychiatric patient who now wants to leave and refuses meds = three legal/ethical threads: DUTY TO WARN/PROTECT (Tarasoff) vs confidentiality; VOLUNTARY→INVOLUNTARY conversion (danger to others); RETAINED patient rights incl. right to refuse non-emergency meds. Also rule out organic/substance cause. RN role: safety (least restrictive) + prevent elopement + escalate to team + support hold + advocate/uphold rights + document.

labs.n: DUTY TO WARN / DUTY TO PROTECT (Tarasoff) + confidentiality limits
labs.v: The TARASOFF doctrine (Tarasoff v. Regents of the University of California, 1976) established that when a patient makes a SERIOUS, CREDIBLE threat of harm to a REASONABLY IDENTIFIABLE third party, the clinician's duty of confidentiality YIELDS to a DUTY TO PROTECT that person — 'the protective privilege ends where the public peril begins.' Reasonable protective steps may include WARNING the intended victim, NOTIFYING LAW ENFORCEMENT, and/or HOSPITALIZING the patient so he cannot act; the specific obligations and immunities vary by state statute, but the principle is widely codified. Confidentiality and HIPAA PERMIT disclosure to prevent serious, imminent harm, but the disclosure must be the MINIMUM NECESSARY to accomplish protection — you warn the identifiable victim and alert authorities; you do NOT broadcast the patient's full psychiatric/medical history to the public. The threat here qualifies: it is specific, includes a plan and means, and names an identifiable victim. The NURSE'S role is to recognize the credible threat, NOT keep it confidential, and immediately escalate to the psychiatrist/provider and team (and risk management/legal per policy) so the duty to warn/protect is carried out and documented; the nurse supports and documents the process and ensures it actually happens. Failing to warn/protect (staying silent 'for confidentiality') and over-disclosing (revealing far more than protection requires) are BOTH errors.
labs.r: TARASOFF = serious, credible threat to a REASONABLY IDENTIFIABLE victim → confidentiality YIELDS to a DUTY TO WARN/PROTECT ('protective privilege ends where public peril begins'): warn the victim and/or notify law enforcement and/or hospitalize. HIPAA permits disclosure to prevent serious/imminent harm, but disclose only the MINIMUM NECESSARY (don't broadcast the whole history). RN role: recognize + escalate to provider/team (don't sit on it) + ensure it's done + document; avoid BOTH silence and over-disclosure.

labs.n: VOLUNTARY vs INVOLUNTARY admission + commitment criteria + RETAINED rights (incl. right to refuse meds)
labs.v: VOLUNTARY admission means the patient consents and generally may request discharge; however, if on evaluation the patient meets COMMITMENT CRITERIA, the facility may place a short legal HOLD instead of releasing them. Civil-commitment criteria (varying by state but sharing a core) are: (1) DANGER TO SELF, (2) DANGER TO OTHERS, or (3) GRAVELY DISABLED due to mental illness — this patient meets DANGER TO OTHERS. An emergency hold (e.g., a 72-hour hold; California's 5150) permits brief involuntary detention and evaluation; CONTINUED commitment requires DUE PROCESS — certification, legal counsel, and a court hearing with periodic review (habeas corpus). CRUCIAL, high-yield point: INVOLUNTARY COMMITMENT ≠ INCOMPETENCE. A committed patient is presumed to RETAIN capacity and RIGHTS: humane care and treatment, the LEAST RESTRICTIVE environment, being informed of their rights, communication (visitors/phone/mail within reasonable limits), legal counsel and periodic judicial review, dignity/privacy, freedom from unnecessary restraint/seclusion, and the RIGHT TO REFUSE non-emergency MEDICATION. Therefore he may refuse his antipsychotic. EMERGENCY medication over objection is allowed ONLY to prevent IMMINENT danger to self/others and must be the least restrictive effective measure; ROUTINE/ongoing medication over objection requires a SEPARATE JUDICIAL order (due process), not merely the hold. The nurse upholds these rights and advocates — offering (not forcing) medication, reserving medication over objection for true emergencies, and supporting the legal process for any ongoing involuntary treatment.
labs.r: VOLUNTARY patient may request discharge BUT can be held if they meet criteria. Commitment criteria: DANGER TO SELF / DANGER TO OTHERS / GRAVELY DISABLED (he = danger to others). Emergency hold (72h; CA 5150) → continued commitment needs DUE PROCESS (certification + counsel + court review/habeas). KEY: commitment ≠ incompetence → retains rights: least restrictive, communication, legal review, dignity, freedom from unnecessary restraint, and RIGHT TO REFUSE non-emergency meds. Forced meds ONLY for IMMINENT danger (emergency, least restrictive) or by a SEPARATE court order. RN role: uphold rights + advocate.

labs.n: Nursing role (safety/least-restrictive + advocacy + documentation) + Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: NURSING MANAGEMENT integrates safety, the duty to protect, and rights. SAFETY first, with the LEAST RESTRICTIVE effective measures: verbal DE-ESCALATION (calm, non-threatening stance, set limits, reduce stimulation), increased/1:1 OBSERVATION, a safe environment, and preventing ELOPEMENT so he cannot leave to act on the threat; restraint/seclusion are reserved for IMMINENT danger when less restrictive measures fail and are themselves time-limited and rights-protected. ESCALATE immediately to the psychiatrist/provider and team (and risk management/legal) so the duty to warn/protect is fulfilled and the hold is properly initiated. ADVOCACY: inform the patient of his rights, honor the right to refuse non-emergency medication (offer PRN, don't force absent imminent danger), preserve communication/legal-review rights, and push for the least restrictive plan. DOCUMENTATION must be OBJECTIVE and CONTEMPORANEOUS: the threat in the patient's own words, the risk assessment, who was notified and when (provider, victim, law enforcement), interventions, the involuntary-hold paperwork, and the patient's rights and his medication refusal. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): the nurse did NOT let a voluntary patient who meets commitment criteria simply walk out; did NOT stay silent about a credible threat to an identifiable victim 'for confidentiality'; did NOT seclude or forcibly medicate him solely because he refused an oral dose (no imminent danger, no court order); did NOT treat the hold as automatic incompetence and override all his choices; and did NOT over-disclose his history beyond what protection required. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: prompt recognition of the credible threat; least-restrictive safety + prevented elopement; immediate escalation so the duty to warn/protect was carried out; an appropriate involuntary hold with due process; upheld retained rights (especially refusal of non-emergency medication); objective documentation; and continued therapeutic engagement leading to voluntary acceptance of treatment. EXPECTED outcome (as here): the victim is protected, the patient is held safely and lawfully with his rights intact, and engagement leads to him accepting treatment.
labs.r: RN management = SAFETY (least restrictive: de-escalate, 1:1 observe, prevent elopement; restraint/seclusion only for imminent danger) + ESCALATE to provider/team (duty to protect + proper hold) + ADVOCACY (inform of rights, honor refusal of non-emergency meds, preserve communication/legal review, least restrictive) + OBJECTIVE/contemporaneous DOCUMENTATION (threat in pt's words, risk assessment, who/when notified, interventions, rights). Negatives confirm correct practice (didn't let him leave, didn't stay silent, didn't seclude/forcibly medicate for a refusal, didn't treat hold as incompetence, didn't over-disclose). Protective: recognize + least-restrictive safety + escalate/warn + lawful hold + upheld rights + documentation + engagement.

labs.v: Negative (no stimulant/PCP/other intoxicant identified as a contributor to the presentation)
2.1 mIU/L (no thyroid contribution to the behavioral change)
96 mg/dL (hypoglycemia excluded as a cause of altered behavior)

nursesNote: Inpatient behavioral health — 34 y/o man, known paranoid schizophrenia, VOLUNTARILY admitted 3 days ago for medication adjustment. During a 1:1 he became agitated/paranoid and voiced a SPECIFIC, credible threat (intent + plan + identified means) to kill a NAMED former coworker he believes is 'controlling his thoughts,' then DEMANDED to leave (voluntary patient) and REFUSED his oral antipsychotic. THREE legal/ethical threads: (1) DUTY TO WARN/PROTECT (Tarasoff) vs confidentiality; (2) VOLUNTARY→INVOLUNTARY conversion (danger to others); (3) RETAINED patient rights incl. right to refuse non-emergency meds. Organic/substance contributors screened out (tox negative, TSH normal, glucose 96). RESPONSE: (1) SAFETY with LEAST RESTRICTIVE measures — verbal de-escalation, 1:1 observation, safe environment, PREVENTED elopement (cannot leave to act on the threat); no restraint/seclusion needed; (2) IMMEDIATELY notified psychiatrist/team (+ risk management/legal) — did NOT keep the credible threat confidential; (3) DUTY TO WARN/PROTECT carried out per law/policy — identifiable victim warned + law enforcement notified, disclosing only the MINIMUM NECESSARY (not the whole history); (4) INVOLUNTARY emergency hold initiated for danger to others (voluntary patient meeting criteria can be held); continued commitment to follow DUE PROCESS (certification + legal counsel + court review/habeas); (5) RIGHTS upheld — patient informed of rights; right to REFUSE non-emergency medication respected (PRN offered + declined; NO imminent danger + NO court order → not forcibly medicated); communication + legal-review rights preserved; commitment ≠ incompetence; (6) OBJECTIVE/contemporaneous documentation (threat in pt's words, risk assessment, who/when notified, interventions, hold paperwork, rights + refusal). NEGATIVES (correct practice): did not let him walk out, did not stay silent, did not seclude/forcibly medicate for a med refusal, did not treat the hold as automatic incompetence, did not over-disclose. PLAN: court certification/hearing scheduled; a SEPARATE court order would be sought only if ongoing refusal + continued danger; continued de-escalation + therapeutic engagement. OUTCOME over days: with engagement he VOLUNTARILY accepted medication, paranoia/threat resolved, victim safety confirmed, rights respected throughout; team debrief held.
providerOrders: ADMIT/STATUS: convert from voluntary to INVOLUNTARY emergency hold for danger to others (e.g., 72-hour hold; CA 5150) per state criteria; initiate certification and legal process for continued commitment with due process (counsel + court hearing/periodic review)
DIAGNOSIS: paranoid schizophrenia with a specific, credible homicidal threat toward a reasonably identifiable third party; danger to others

DUTY TO WARN/PROTECT (Tarasoff): treatment team/provider to warn the identifiable intended victim and notify law enforcement per state law/facility policy; disclose only the minimum necessary to protect; document who was notified and when (involve risk management/legal)
Verbal de-escalation; reduce environmental stimulation; set clear, calm limits
1:1 (continuous) observation; prevent elopement; ensure unit/staff/patient safety; search for and remove means of harm per policy
Restraint/seclusion ONLY for imminent danger when less restrictive measures fail — time-limited, monitored, rights-protected; not for refusing medication and not for convenience
Offer the prescribed antipsychotic and PRN medication; the patient RETAINS the right to refuse non-emergency medication (commitment is not incompetence) — do not force
Emergency medication over objection ONLY to prevent imminent danger to self/others, least restrictive, fully documented
For ongoing involuntary medication absent an emergency, obtain a SEPARATE court order (due process); psychiatry to evaluate decision-making capacity if in question
Inform the patient of his rights; provide access to legal counsel and periodic judicial review (habeas corpus)
Preserve communication rights (visitors/phone/mail within reasonable, documented limits), dignity, and privacy; use the least restrictive environment; act as patient advocate
ASSESSMENT/WORKUP: psychiatric risk assessment (intent/plan/means/identifiable target); rule out organic/substance contributors (urine toxicology, TSH, glucose/metabolic) — all unremarkable here

DOCUMENTATION: objective, contemporaneous record — the threat in the patient's own words, risk assessment, notifications (provider, victim, law enforcement) with times, interventions, hold paperwork, the patient's rights, and the medication refusal

TEAM/AFTERCARE: interdisciplinary coordination (psychiatry, nursing, social work, risk management/legal, patient-rights advocate); ongoing therapeutic engagement; reassess danger/hold status; plan for least restrictive care and eventual step-down; team debrief

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters about this patient's threat and status?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: He voices a SPECIFIC, credible threat — intent, a plan, and identified means — to kill a clearly IDENTIFIABLE former coworker
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — a specific, credible threat to an identifiable person is the central, actionable finding. RN role: recognize + escalate.

opts.text: A credible threat to an identifiable victim triggers the DUTY TO WARN/PROTECT (Tarasoff), an exception to confidentiality
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — confidentiality yields to the duty to protect an identifiable third party. RN role: recognize the duty + escalate so it is carried out.

opts.text: He now meets involuntary-hold criteria as a DANGER TO OTHERS and cannot simply be allowed to leave despite his voluntary admission
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — a voluntary patient who meets commitment criteria can be held. RN role: prevent elopement + support the hold.

opts.text: He is refusing his antipsychotic; involuntary commitment does NOT by itself remove his right to refuse non-emergency medication
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — commitment is not incompetence; the refusal must be respected absent an emergency or court order. RN role: uphold the right + advocate.

opts.text: Safety: he is agitated, pacing, and paranoid — use least-restrictive measures (de-escalation, 1:1 observation) and protect everyone's safety
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — safety with the least restrictive effective measures is foundational. RN role: de-escalate + observe + keep safe.

opts.text: Assess for and help rule out a treatable/organic or substance-related contributor (intoxication/withdrawal, metabolic)
opts.rat: Relevant — organic/substance causes can drive dangerous behavior and must be excluded. Supports the workup.

opts.text: Because his admission was voluntary, honor his request to leave immediately and tell no one, in order to protect his confidentiality
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. A voluntary patient who meets commitment criteria can be held, and a credible threat to an identifiable victim must be escalated and protected against — not concealed.

opts.text: Place him in four-point restraints and forcibly inject the antipsychotic now solely because he refused his oral dose
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. A medication refusal alone — with no imminent danger and no court order — does not justify restraint or forced medication; use the least restrictive measure.

opts.text: The team has a dual obligation: to protect/treat the patient AND to take reasonable steps to protect an identifiable potential victim (duty to warn/protect)
opts.rat: CORRECT — the dual duty is the core of the case. Foundation for action.

opts.text: Confidentiality yields when there is a serious, credible threat to an identifiable person; disclosure is limited to what is necessary to protect
opts.rat: CORRECT — the Tarasoff/confidentiality balance, including minimum-necessary disclosure. Foundation for proper warning.

opts.text: Civil-commitment criteria are danger to self, danger to others, or gravely disabled due to mental illness; he meets 'danger to others'
opts.rat: CORRECT — the commitment criteria. Foundation for the involuntary hold.

opts.text: A voluntary patient may request discharge, but may be held if they meet commitment criteria; he is converted to an involuntary hold
opts.rat: CORRECT — voluntary-to-involuntary conversion. Foundation for not releasing a dangerous patient.

opts.text: An involuntarily committed patient retains rights — least-restrictive environment, communication, legal counsel/review, dignity, and the right to REFUSE non-emergency medication (commitment is not incompetence)
opts.rat: CORRECT — retained rights, the high-yield point. Foundation for advocacy.

opts.text: Medication over objection is permissible ONLY for imminent danger (emergency, least restrictive); ongoing involuntary medication requires a separate court order (due process)
opts.rat: CORRECT — the emergency vs court-order distinction for forced medication. Foundation for respecting the refusal.

opts.text: An involuntary hold automatically makes the patient incompetent and authorizes any medication or treatment over his objection
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Commitment does not equal incompetence; the patient retains the right to refuse non-emergency treatment, which requires a separate judicial process to override.

opts.text: The duty to warn means the nurse should disclose the patient's entire psychiatric and medical history to anyone who asks
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Disclosure must be the minimum necessary to protect the identifiable victim — over-disclosure violates confidentiality.

opts.text: Ensure immediate safety using the least restrictive effective measures (de-escalate, increase observation) and prevent him from leaving to act on the threat
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Safety and preventing elopement protect both the patient and the potential victim right now. RN role: least-restrictive safety + prevent elopement.

opts.text: Immediately notify the psychiatrist/provider and team of the specific, credible threat (do not keep it confidential)
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Escalation triggers the duty to warn/protect and the hold; a credible threat must not be sat on. RN role: report + escalate.

opts.text: Initiate the involuntary hold (danger to others) so he cannot leave to harm the identifiable victim
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Converting to an involuntary hold is the lawful basis for detaining a dangerous patient. RN role: support/initiate the hold.

opts.text: Ensure the duty to warn/protect is carried out — warn the identifiable victim and/or notify law enforcement per law/policy
opts.rat: #4 — Fulfills the duty to protect the third party, disclosing only what is necessary. RN role: ensure the warning happens + document.

opts.text: Uphold the patient's retained rights (least restrictive, refuse non-emergency medication, communication, legal review) and document objectively
opts.rat: #5 — Advocacy and documentation run throughout and protect the patient's rights and the record. RN role: advocate + document.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this situation.

opts.text: De-escalate verbally and use the least restrictive measures to maintain safety; increase to 1:1 observation and prevent elopement
opts.rat: Least-restrictive safety protects everyone and prevents him from acting on the threat. RN role: de-escalate + observe.

opts.text: Immediately notify the psychiatrist/provider and treatment team about the specific, credible threat
opts.rat: Escalation triggers the duty to warn/protect and proper hold; a credible threat must be reported. RN role: escalate.

opts.text: Prevent him from leaving and initiate an involuntary hold because he is a danger to others
opts.rat: A voluntary patient who meets commitment criteria can be held. RN role: support/initiate the hold.

opts.text: Ensure the duty to warn/protect is carried out — warn the identifiable victim and/or notify law enforcement, disclosing only what is necessary
opts.rat: Fulfills the duty to protect the third party with minimum-necessary disclosure. RN role: ensure the warning + document.

opts.text: Inform the patient of his rights and uphold his right to refuse non-emergency medication; pursue a court order if ongoing involuntary medication is needed
opts.rat: Commitment is not incompetence; the refusal stands absent an emergency, and due process is required to override it. RN role: advocate + uphold rights.

opts.text: Offer (not force) PRN medication, continue therapeutic engagement, and document objectively
opts.rat: Offering rather than forcing respects autonomy while keeping the door open; objective documentation protects all parties. RN role: offer + engage + document.

opts.text: Assess for and help rule out organic or substance-related contributors to the behavior
opts.rat: Excluding treatable medical/substance causes is part of a sound assessment. RN role: contribute to the workup.

opts.text: Honor his request to leave AMA immediately and tell no one, in order to protect confidentiality
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — a dangerous patient who meets criteria cannot be released, and a credible threat to an identifiable victim must be escalated and protected against.

opts.text: Place him in seclusion/restraints and forcibly inject medication solely because he refused his oral dose, with no imminent assault
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — a refusal alone, absent imminent danger and a court order, does not justify seclusion/restraint or forced medication; this is not the least restrictive measure.

opts.text: Treat the involuntary hold as making him incompetent and give all medications and treatments over his objection without due process
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — commitment does not equal incompetence; overriding non-emergency treatment requires a separate judicial determination.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Maintain safety with least-restrictive de-escalation, increase observation, and prevent him from leaving while summoning help
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Immediate safety and preventing elopement protect the patient and the potential victim. RN role: least-restrictive safety + prevent elopement.

opts.text: Immediately notify the psychiatrist/provider and team of the specific, credible threat
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Escalation triggers the duty to warn/protect and the hold. RN role: report + escalate.

opts.text: Initiate the involuntary hold (danger to others) and ensure the duty to warn/protect is carried out (victim warned and/or law enforcement notified) per law/policy
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Lawfully detain the dangerous patient and protect the identifiable third party with minimum-necessary disclosure. RN role: support hold + ensure warning.

opts.text: Inform the patient of his rights and uphold the right to refuse non-emergency medication (offer, don't force; emergency meds only for imminent danger); seek a court order for ongoing involuntary treatment if needed
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Advocacy and due process protect the rights commitment does not remove. RN role: uphold rights + advocate.

opts.text: Document objectively and contemporaneously, advocate for the least-restrictive plan, and support ongoing engagement and legal review
opts.rat: #5 — Throughout/after. Objective documentation and continued advocacy/engagement complete the response. RN role: document + advocate + engage.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each action/outcome, classify as APPROPRIATE or INAPPROPRIATE.

opts.text: The nurse de-escalated, increased observation, and prevented elopement while notifying the psychiatrist — safety was maintained without unnecessary restraint
opts.rat: Least-restrictive safety + prompt escalation is exactly the correct response. Protects patient and potential victim.

opts.text: The team carried out the duty to warn/protect — warned the identifiable victim and notified law enforcement — disclosing only what was necessary
opts.rat: Fulfills the Tarasoff duty with minimum-necessary disclosure. Correct balance of confidentiality and protection.

opts.text: He was converted to an involuntary hold for danger to others, informed of his rights, and his right to refuse non-emergency medication was respected pending due process
opts.rat: Lawful detention of a dangerous patient while upholding retained rights. Commitment is not incompetence.

opts.text: The nurse documented the threat in the patient's words, the risk assessment, who was notified and when, interventions, and the patient's rights — objectively and contemporaneously
opts.rat: Objective, timely documentation protects the patient, the team, and the record. Standard of practice.

opts.text: Alternative — the nurse let him leave AMA without notifying anyone 'to protect confidentiality'; he later approached the named victim
opts.rat: ⚠️ Releasing a dangerous patient who meets criteria and failing to warn breaches both safety and the duty to protect. Demonstrates the central error to avoid.

opts.text: Alternative — the nurse stayed silent about the threat because of confidentiality and did not warn anyone or notify the provider
opts.rat: ⚠️ Confidentiality yields to a credible threat to an identifiable victim; silence violates the duty to warn/protect.

opts.text: Alternative — he was secluded and forcibly medicated solely for refusing his oral dose, with no imminent danger and no court order
opts.rat: ⚠️ A refusal alone does not justify seclusion or forced medication; this is neither an emergency nor court-authorized and is not least restrictive.

opts.text: Alternative — staff treated the involuntary hold as automatic incompetence and gave all routine medications over his objection without due process
opts.rat: ⚠️ Commitment is not incompetence; overriding non-emergency treatment requires a separate judicial determination.

opts.text: Alternative — the duty to warn was 'carried out' by disclosing his entire psychiatric and medical history to the victim and to the media
opts.rat: ⚠️ Disclosure must be the minimum necessary to protect; broadcasting the full history violates confidentiality.

opts.text: Over days of engagement he voluntarily accepted medication, the paranoia and threat resolved, and the victim's safety was confirmed — with his rights respected throughout
opts.rat: The ideal outcome: lawful, least-restrictive, rights-respecting care leading to voluntary treatment and resolution of risk.
